figures.caption: Table 2: Image generation results on GenEval, DPG-Bench, and WISE. † refers to methods using LLM rewriters on GenEval. Abbreviations for WISE: Cult. (Cultural), Bio. (Biology), Phy. (Physics), Chem. (Chemistry).

Figure 15: More results on intelligent image editing. These examples require complex world knowledge such as biological transformations, geographical understanding, and cultural context to perform accurate editing.

Figure 3: Comparison of the results for image editing, highlighting UniCom performance in tasks such as image manipulation, object swapping, and color adjustment. See more visualization in the Appendix.

Figure 16: Chain-of-thought enhanced generation and editing. For each example, we show the generated image along with the corresponding thinking process enclosed in <think> tokens. The model first reasons about the required visual transformation, then generates the output image accordingly.

Figure 8: T-SNE visualization of feature distributions. We randomly select 6 distinct classes from ImageNet, sampling 150 images per class. MHA compression to 64-d preserves the structure, MLP projection yields scattered 64-d features, and FLUX-VAE has no semantic separability due to pixel-reconstruction focus.

Figure 1: High-quality samples generated by UniCom. Built on compressed continuous representations, our unified multimodal model demonstrates exceptional capabilities in text-to-image generation, precise image editing, and fine-grained controllable generation.
Figure 12: Our unified prediction architecture (UniCom) preserves finer spatial layout and structural consistency, while query-based methods struggle with spatial correspondence and editing fidelity.

Figure 4: Overview of the proposed diffusion decoder and reconstruction analysis. Given an input image, the vision encoder extracts semantic features which are then processed by compressor and decompressor to condition the diffusion model for image reconstruction. During training, the compression modules and the diffusion backbone are optimized, while the encoder remains frozen. We investigate the bottleneck capacity by varying the token number n and feature dimension d. As illustrated in the bottom row, compressing the dimension d yields superior reconstruction fidelity, whereas reducing the token number n leads to noticeable blurring in fine details.

Figure 5: Visual comparison of image reconstruction results. Notably, our method (d64) maintains high fidelity in high-frequency details (e.g., text characters) and preserves facial identity better than these semantic-based baselines, achieving quality comparable to the specialized Flux.1-dev VAE.

Figure 2: Overview of the proposed framework. For a controlled comparison, both pathways are built upon the same compressed representations and jointly optimized with cross-entropy loss (Lce) and flow matching loss (L f m).
Table 7: The performance of both commercial and open-source models on image editing tasks in WorldEdit-Test, evaluated across different causes and metrics.

Figure 11: Qualitative reconstruction samples using Decoder (d64). Left: Reconstructed image. Right: Original image. Even with an 18× channel compression ratio, our model accurately recovers complex high-frequency details.
Figure 10: Additional visual comparison of image reconstruction results.

Table 3: Comparison of image editing capabilities. We evaluate on ImgEdit-Bench, GEdit-Bench, KRIS-Bench and WorldEdit. For ImgEdit-Bench, performance is evaluated across nine distinct operation categories (e.g., ‘Add’, ‘Adjust’, ‘Extract’, ‘Replace’, ‘Remove’, ‘Background’, ‘Style’, ‘Hybrid’, and ‘Action’. ). For GEdit-Bench, metrics include ‘G-Semantic Consistency’ (G-SC) and ‘G-Perceptual Quality’ (G-PQ). For KRIS-Bench, we report Factual (Fact.), Conceptual (Conc.), and Procedural (Proc.) knowledge scores. Bold: best results. Underline: second-best.
Figure 14: More results on multi-element composition. Our model can compose multiple reference subjects into a coherent scene while preserving the identity of each element.

Figure 13: More results on single image editing. From top to bottom: (1) Add/Remove/Extract: adding objects, removing elements, and extracting subjects; (2) Replace: substituting objects or materials while preserving context; (3) Style Transfer: transforming image appearance into different artistic styles; (4) Background: modifying or replacing image backgrounds; (5) Subject-Driven Generation: generating new images conditioned on reference subjects; (6) Controllable Generation: generating images with spatial or structural control signals.

Table 1: Evaluation on image reconstruction ability on the ImageNet validation set. d denotes the feature dimension of compressed representations z̃. Models marked with † are re-evaluated using official checkpoints.

Figure 9: Reducing dimensions maintains higher reconstruction fidelity than sequence reduction. We compare the reconstruction training dynamics (Loss and PSNR) of different compressed representations (z̃).
